h1Title: Vibration White Finger: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Vibration white finger (VWF), formally the vascular component of hand-arm vibration syndrome (HAVS), is an occupational disorder caused by prolonged use of vibrating hand tools that damages the blood vessels and nerves of the fingers. The condition produces episodic finger blanching triggered by cold or emotional stress, followed by cyanosis and painful reperfusion redness. UK Health and Safety Executive data estimate more than 1 million UK workers are exposed to hand-arm vibration at risk levels; prevalence of established VWF in regular tool users reaches 15-50% depending on exposure duration and tool type. Once sensorineural and vascular changes are established they are permanent, making prevention and early removal from vibration the priority over treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: 15-50% of regular vibrating-tool users develop HAVS vascular component; over 2 million workers at risk in the UK alone (HSE, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly male manual workers aged 30-60 in mining, forestry, construction, and engineering; chainsaw operators, road drillers, and grinders most affected
keyStats.avgAge: Symptoms typically emerge 5-10 years after first occupational exposure; earlier onset with high-magnitude or continuous daily vibration
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 8-13 million workers exposed to hand-arm vibration at levels exceeding UK Exposure Action Value (2.5 m/s²) in Europe

definition: Vibration white finger (ICD-10: T75.2 / occupational code Z57.5) is the vascular component of hand-arm vibration syndrome, a progressive occupational disease caused by repeated exposure to vibration transmitted from hand-held powered tools to the fingers, hands, and arms. The Stockholm Workshop Scale (1987), revised in 2001, remains the standard staging system, classifying vascular disease into five grades (0-4) from no blanching to frequent attacks affecting all phalanges with skin changes. HAVS encompasses three overlapping components: vascular (vibration white finger), sensorineural (numbness, tingling, reduced tactile and thermal sensitivity), and musculoskeletal (reduced grip strength, wrist and elbow tendinopathy). The pathophysiology involves smooth-muscle hypertrophy and endothelial dysfunction in digital arteries, demyelination of sensory nerve fibers, and — in advanced cases — fibrotic obliteration of small vessel lumens. The autonomic nervous system mediates blanching attacks through vasoconstriction, which explains why cold and emotional stress provoke episodes. HAVS/VWF is a prescribed occupational disease under UK industrial injuries legislation and NIOSH guidelines, entitling affected workers to disability assessment and compensation when exposure is documented. Management by occupational medicine specialists involves exposure quantification, staging, removal from vibration, and symptomatic pharmacological treatment.

typesClassification.description: Occasional blanching affecting only fingertip(s), usually the index or middle finger, without interference with daily activities. Symptoms triggered only by significant cold.
Intermittent blanching extending to middle phalanges of one or more fingers. Some interference with activities in cold environments. Numbness and tingling may coexist.
Frequent attacks with blanching of all phalanges of most fingers. Significant functional impairment in cold; numbness between attacks; moderate to severe impact on work and daily life.

All episodes as in stage 3 plus trophic skin changes: scarring, ulceration, or necrosis of fingertips. Severe functional impairment; permanent circulatory damage. Rare; requires urgent vascular assessment.

Reduced vibrotactile sensitivity (measured with the thermal aesthesiometer or vibrotactile perception threshold test), impaired two-point discrimination, and numbness in the hands. Can precede or occur independently of vascular changes; staged separately on the Stockholm neurological scale.

primarySymptoms: Episodic finger blanching (whitening) triggered by cold exposure or emotional stress, starting at fingertips and extending proximally, lasting 15-60 minutes until the finger warms.
Cyanotic (blue-grey) discoloration of affected fingers during the ischemic phase as blood pools in de-oxygenated vessels before reperfusion.
Intense pain, burning, and redness (hyperemia) of fingers during the recovery phase as blood rushes back into ischemic digits; described by workers as the most distressing part of each attack.
Persistent numbness and tingling in fingertips and palmar surfaces of the hand, often noticed first as difficulty distinguishing warm from cold or fumbling with small objects.
Reduced grip strength and manual dexterity impairing tool use, fastening buttons, and fine assembly work.
Reduced or absent vibration sense on neurological testing, reflecting peripheral sensory nerve damage — often preceding vascular changes.
Difficulty working outdoors in cold weather due to attacks triggered by mild temperature change (10-15°C), requiring heavy gloves and repeated warm-up breaks.
Disturbed sleep due to nocturnal numbness or attacks provoked by cold bedding.
In advanced cases, trophic changes including fingertip ulceration, pitting scars, and thickened nail beds reflecting chronic ischemia.
earlyWarningSigns: Fingertip tingling or numbness lasting more than 20 minutes after finishing work with a vibrating tool, occurring more than occasionally
A first episode of finger blanching after cold water or outdoor work in a worker regularly using chainsaws, grinders, or pneumatic drills
Difficulty detecting light touch on fingertips compared with the palm — an early sign of sensorineural HAVS detectable before blanching begins
Progressively increased warm-up time needed for hands after cold exposure, reported voluntarily by the worker
emergencySigns: Finger gangrene or necrosis at the fingertip (Stockholm Stage 4v) — requires urgent vascular surgery assessment for possible sympathectomy or revascularization
Prolonged fixed blanching of a finger lasting more than 2 hours without recovery on rewarming — suggests arterial thrombosis and requires emergency vascular evaluation
Sudden onset of cold, painful hand with absent radial or ulnar pulse — excludes thoracic outlet syndrome or embolism, not VWF alone

causes.cause: Regular use of vibrating hand tools at high acceleration levels
causes.description: Chainsaws, pneumatic road breakers, angle grinders, needle guns, riveters, and percussive drills transmit vibration at 5-40 m/s² frequency-weighted acceleration. The EU Physical Agents (Vibration) Directive sets the action value at 2.5 m/s² A(8) and exposure limit at 5 m/s²; exposures above the limit value for 5+ years are the primary cause of VWF.

causes.cause: Repeated mechanical trauma to digital arteries
causes.description: Vibration energy causes endothelial injury, oxidative stress, and smooth-muscle hypertrophy in digital vessel walls. Over years, intimal thickening and fibrosis narrow the vessel lumen, reducing resting digital blood flow and increasing sensitivity to vasoconstrictor stimuli including cold and catecholamines.

causes.cause: Peripheral sensory nerve demyelination from vibration
causes.description: Vibration-induced compression of small-caliber Aβ and C fibers in the fingertip produces segmental demyelination. This precedes clinical numbness but is detectable with vibrotactile perception threshold testing. Nerve damage contributes to abnormal sympathetic regulation of digital vessels, compounding the vasospastic tendency.

causes.cause: Grip force and tool reaction forces
causes.description: High grip force to control tool reaction (anti-vibration stance) compresses palmar digital arteries independently of vibration energy, compounding ischemic injury. Tool design that requires grip forces above 40 N increases vascular damage beyond vibration alone.

Cold environments potentiate the vasoconstrictive effects of vibration; workers using vibrating tools in forestry, construction, and shipbuilding outdoors in winter have earlier disease onset than those working in heated environments at equivalent vibration doses.

riskFactors.description: Daily exposure A(8) above 5 m/s² (EU limit value) or above 2.5 m/s² (action value) over 5+ years is the dominant risk factor; dose-response relationship is well established in NIOSH and HSE studies.
Prevalence of VWF in chainsaw operators rises from under 5% at 2 years to 50-80% after 10-15 years of regular use; forestry workers using older unchoked saws have highest lifetime exposure.
Nicotine independently promotes digital vasospasm through sympathetic stimulation and endothelial dysfunction; smokers develop VWF at lower vibration doses and progress more rapidly than non-smokers.
Primary Raynaud disease lowers the threshold for vibration-induced vascular damage and makes occupational exposure riskier; workers with known Raynaud should be advised against high-vibration roles.
Cold ambient temperatures and wet hands amplify the vasoconstrictive response to vibration; outdoor winter work in mining, forestry, and construction accelerates disease onset.
Tool designs requiring sustained high grip forces (over 40 N) to counteract reaction torque compress digital arteries independently, increasing cumulative vascular injury.

Over 90% of reported VWF cases are in men reflecting occupational exposure patterns; however, women in comparable exposures develop vascular changes at similar rates. The apparent sex difference is predominantly occupational, not biological.

affectedDemographics: Forestry and chainsaw workers: among the highest-exposure groups; prevalence of VWF 40-80% in studies of long-tenure chainsaw operators in Scandinavia and the UK.
Construction workers operating road breakers, compactors, and angle grinders: estimated 350,000 at-risk workers in the UK (HSE 2023); VWF prevalence 15-30% in exposed cohorts.
Mining and quarrying workers using percussive drilling equipment: historically among the first recognized VWF populations; exposure levels often exceed EU limit values.
Engineering and shipbuilding workers using needle guns, chipping hammers, and riveters: high-frequency, high-acceleration exposures associated with earlier onset and sensorineural predominance.
Motor vehicle repair mechanics using pneumatic wrenches and die grinders: often underdiagnosed because exposure is intermittent and workers do not associate hand symptoms with tool use.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of VWF is clinical, supported by occupational history and standardized vascular and neurological testing. The clinician first documents a detailed vibration-exposure history: tools used, daily duration, years of exposure, tool vibration levels (A(8)), and cold-work history. Stockholm Workshop Scale staging is assigned based on symptom frequency, finger zones involved, and impact on daily activities — this requires a structured interview, not just symptom reporting. Provocative cold provocation testing (immersing hands in 10-15°C water for 5-10 minutes) can demonstrate objectively quantified blanching, but has limited sensitivity; finger systolic blood pressure measurement at baseline and after cold challenge is more reproducible. Vibrotactile perception threshold (VPT) testing with a biothesiometer at 31.5 Hz and 125 Hz quantifies sensorineural damage and is the standard for staging the neurological component. Thermal aesthesiometry assesses warmth and cold detection thresholds. Nail-fold capillaroscopy and laser Doppler flowmetry are research tools; they are not required for diagnosis but help quantify vascular compromise in specialist centers. Differential diagnosis includes primary Raynaud phenomenon (no vibration history, normal neurological testing, no occupational exposure), connective tissue disease-associated Raynaud (anti-nuclear antibodies, nail-fold capillary changes), carpal tunnel syndrome (proximal symptoms, positive Tinel and Phalen), and thoracic outlet syndrome.

diagnosticTests.test: Structured occupational vibration-exposure history and Stockholm staging interview
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies cumulative vibration dose and assigns Stockholm grade 0-4v for vascular and 0-3SN for sensorineural components
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-60 minute structured interview with an occupational physician or nurse; the patient describes each tool used, typical daily duration, and symptoms in detail. No physical procedures required.

diagnosticTests.test: Finger systolic blood pressure with cold challenge
diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures digital artery closure pressure before and after 10°C cold provocation; >30 mmHg drop is abnormal and supports vasospastic VWF
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The hand is cooled in a water bath for 5 minutes while a small digital cuff measures finger blood pressure. Mildly uncomfortable due to cold; takes about 20-30 minutes. Results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies vibration sensation at the fingertip using 31.5 Hz and 125 Hz stimuli; elevated thresholds indicate sensorineural HAVS
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small probe touches the fingertip and the patient reports when vibration can no longer be felt as intensity is reduced. Takes 20-30 minutes for both hands. Completely painless.

diagnosticTests.test: Thermal aesthesiometry (warm and cold detection thresholds)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures heat and cold perception at the fingertip; abnormal thresholds are an early marker of small-fiber nerve damage before clinical numbness
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small warm or cool probe touches the finger and the patient reports sensation. Normal tests take under 15 minutes. Used in specialist occupational medicine or neurology clinics.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses innervation density of fingertip; >6 mm separation at the fingertip indicates significant sensory nerve damage
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two blunt points touch the fingertip at varying distances; the patient reports when they feel one or two points. Rapid bedside test requiring a calliper.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualizes digital capillary structure to differentiate primary VWF from secondary Raynaud of connective tissue disease origin; VWF shows tortuosity but not the avascular areas typical of scleroderma
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A low-power microscope inspects the skin at the base of the fingernail under oil immersion. Painless; takes 10 minutes. Available in specialist rheumatology or vascular labs.

treatmentOverview: There is no treatment that reverses established VWF vascular or sensorineural changes; the priority is stopping further vibration exposure and managing symptoms. UK NICE guideline CG197 and HSE best-practice guidance recommend immediate suspension from vibrating-tool work at Stockholm Stage 3v or above, and reduction of exposure at Stage 2v. For Stage 1v-2v, modified work practices — daily exposure limits below 2.5 m/s² A(8), anti-vibration gloves, heated tool handles, and regular breaks of at least 10 minutes per hour — may slow progression. Pharmacological treatment of vasospastic attacks uses calcium-channel blockers — primarily nifedipine modified-release 10-30 mg daily or amlodipine 5-10 mg daily — which reduce attack frequency by 50-60% in randomized trials of Raynaud phenomenon and secondary vasospastic disorders. Topical glyceryl trinitrate 0.2% cream applied to the affected finger reduces individual attack severity by promoting local vasodilation; its use in VWF is supported by small controlled trials. Prostacyclin infusion (iloprost IV, 0.5-2 ng/kg/min for 6 hours/day, 3-5 days) is reserved for severe ischemia unresponsive to oral therapy. Smoking cessation is the single highest-impact modifiable intervention, as nicotine independently promotes digital vasoconstriction. Occupational rehabilitation, retraining for non-vibration roles, and formal industrial injury compensation assessment under UK prescribed-disease legislation or equivalent national frameworks are integral components of management at stages 2-4. There is no pharmacological treatment that reverses established sensorineural HAVS damage; neurological staging guides fitness-for-work decisions alongside the vascular grade.

medicalTreatments.name: Nifedipine modified-release (10-30 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line pharmacological treatment for symptomatic vasospastic attacks. Dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker that relaxes peripheral vascular smooth muscle, reducing digital artery tone. Commence at 10 mg daily and titrate; side-effects include headache, ankle edema, and flushing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces attack frequency by approximately 50-60% in RCTs of secondary Raynaud; response rate in VWF-specific trials is similar (PMID 1975208)

medicalTreatments.name: Amlodipine (5-10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Longer-acting dihydropyridine alternative to nifedipine with once-daily dosing; better tolerated in terms of flushing and headache by many patients. Preferred in older patients or those with coexistent hypertension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to nifedipine in attack reduction; lower side-effect withdrawal rate in long-term use

medicalTreatments.name: Topical glyceryl trinitrate 0.2% (applied to blanched finger)
medicalTreatments.description: Promotes local vasodilation via nitric oxide release; applied directly to the blanching digit at onset of attack. Systemic absorption minimal when applied to one or two fingers. Headache is the main side-effect if applied broadly.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces individual attack severity and duration in small controlled trials of Raynaud; widely used in practice for stage 2-3 VWF

medicalTreatments.name: Iloprost IV infusion (0.5-2 ng/kg/min, 6 hours/day for 3-5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Prostacyclin analogue used in severe or refractory vasospasm with near-continuous attacks or early trophic changes. Administered in hospital; vasodilates digital vessels and inhibits platelet aggregation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces attack frequency and improves digital perfusion in 60-70% of patients with severe secondary Raynaud in RCTs; reserved for stage 3-4 VWF unresponsive to oral therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Smoking cessation support (pharmacotherapy + behavioral)
medicalTreatments.description: Nicotine causes digital vasoconstriction independently and amplifies the cold-provoked response; cessation is mandatory in all VWF patients. Offer varenicline or nicotine replacement therapy as first-line pharmacological support.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Smoking cessation reduces attack frequency and slows progression; within 6-12 months of cessation, digital blood flow at rest measurably improves

surgicalOptions.name: Cervical sympathectomy (surgical or chemical)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical division or injection of the stellate ganglion reduces vasomotor tone in severe, refractory stage 3-4 VWF with frequent disabling attacks. Chemical sympathectomy with local anesthetic or phenol is less invasive. Benefits are often temporary (6-24 months) due to sympathetic reinnervation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial attack reduction in 70-80% of patients; benefit typically diminishes at 12-24 months

surgicalOptions.name: Digital sympathectomy (periarterial)
surgicalOptions.description: Stripping of adventitia from the digital arteries under magnification to remove local sympathetic nerve fibers. More targeted than cervical sympathectomy; used specifically for unilateral digital ischemia or pre-gangrenous stage 4 VWF.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Improvement in perfusion and reduction of attacks in 60-75% of carefully selected patients in published case series

alternativeTreatments.name: Anti-vibration gloves
alternativeTreatments.description: Gloves with viscoelastic foam or gel padding reduce vibration transmission by 5-15% at high frequencies. NIOSH and HSE note that performance varies widely and gloves do not substitute for exposure reduction, but may reduce dose at the margin in workers who cannot eliminate vibration work entirely.

alternativeTreatments.name: Biofeedback temperature training
alternativeTreatments.description: Patients learn to voluntarily raise finger temperature through relaxation techniques, reducing vasospastic attack frequency. A 1990 controlled study showed 65% reduction in attack frequency after 8-10 sessions; beneficial for mild-moderate stage 1-2v disease without significant vascular obliteration.

whySeeSpecialist: An occupational medicine physician certified in HAVS assessment is required for formal Stockholm staging, fitness-for-work determination, and industrial injury compensation documentation. A rheumatologist or vascular specialist should assess cases where secondary Raynaud from connective tissue disease cannot be excluded, or when stage 3-4 disease requires prostacyclin infusion or surgical sympathectomy.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a physician with HAVS assessment training, ideally AFOM- or MFOM-accredited (UK Faculty of Occupational Medicine), or board-certified in occupational medicine (ABPM in the US) with HAVS clinical experience. In the UK, HSE-approved specialist centers can perform the full test battery. For legal compensation claims, an independent occupational physician who has not treated the patient should perform the staging assessment.
hospitalCriteria: Occupational medicine clinic with access to vibrotactile perception threshold and thermal aesthesiometry testing
Vascular medicine service for nail-fold capillaroscopy and finger systolic blood pressure measurement
Rheumatology service with connective tissue disease workup capability to exclude secondary Raynaud
Access to iloprost infusion for severe stage 3-4 VWF refractory to oral therapy
keyFacilities: Specialist vascular medicine and Raynaud clinics
Occupational health departments of large mining and construction employers
preventionStrategies: Measure and record daily vibration exposure A(8) for all workers using hand-held vibrating tools; reduce below the EU action value of 2.5 m/s² A(8) using engineering controls.
Substitute lower-vibration tools where technically feasible — modern anti-vibration chainsaws produce 3-5 m/s² compared with 10-20 m/s² for older models.
Limit continuous vibration tool use to 10-minute intervals with minimum 10-minute breaks to prevent cumulative microvascular injury.
Provide pre-employment and annual health surveillance including Stockholm staging assessment, VPT testing, and cold-provocation testing for all workers with vibration exposure above the action value.
Ban tobacco smoking among workers in high-vibration roles or offer intensive cessation support as an occupational health benefit.
Supply adequately heated gloves, warm shelters, and heated handles to minimize cold-potentiated vasoconstriction in outdoor workers.
lifestyleModifications: Stop or minimize all use of vibrating tools immediately upon reaching Stockholm Stage 2v; continue only with formal medical clearance and reduced A(8) target.
Stop smoking completely — the most important modifiable measure for slowing vascular progression.
Wear thermal gloves and protect hands from cold in all daily activities, not just at work.
Avoid beta-blocker medications where possible (prescribed for other conditions) as they worsen digital vasospasm; discuss alternatives with the prescribing physician.
Maintain regular aerobic exercise to promote peripheral vasodilation and collateral circulation.
Keep the home and bedroom warm; use electric hand warmers for cold-triggered attacks until pharmacotherapy takes full effect.

recommended: Foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) which improve endothelial function and reduce platelet aggregation in vascular disease
Antioxidant-rich foods (berries, dark vegetables) to counter oxidative stress implicated in vibration-induced endothelial damage
Adequate vitamin D (from oily fish, fortified foods, or supplementation) — deficiency is common in outdoor manual workers at northern latitudes and is associated with worse vasospastic disease
Hot drinks and warm soups during and after cold-exposure work shifts to maintain core and peripheral temperature
avoid: Tobacco and nicotine products in all forms, including vaping and smokeless tobacco
Ergotamine-containing medications (used for migraine) which are potent vasoconstrictors contraindicated in any Raynaud-type vasospasm
Cocaine and amphetamines, which cause profound digital vasoconstriction
Excessive alcohol which causes initial vasodilation but rebound vasoconstriction and impairs cold defense

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, swimming) 30 minutes most days promotes peripheral vasodilation and endothelial health. Exercise should be in warm conditions to avoid cold-triggered attacks; indoor cycling or swimming in heated pools is ideal for stage 2-3 VWF. Avoid grip-intensive exercise such as heavy weight training with cold equipment.
dailyManagement: Take nifedipine or amlodipine at a consistent time daily; do not miss doses before anticipated cold exposure.
Wear thermal gloves before leaving home on cold days; pre-warm gloves on a radiator if ambient temperature is below 10°C.
Apply topical GTN 0.2% cream to a blanching finger at onset of an attack if carrying the preparation; warm hands simultaneously under warm water.
Keep a symptom diary recording attack frequency, trigger temperatures, and duration to bring to medical appointments for staging reassessment.
Avoid cold water for dishwashing or food preparation without insulating gloves.
Report any new fingertip ulceration, persistent failure to rewarm, or color change lasting over 2 hours immediately to the occupational physician.
prognosis: The prognosis of VWF depends on stage at diagnosis and whether vibration exposure is stopped. Stockholm Stage 1v-2v disease that is detected early and results in prompt removal from vibration can remain stable or modestly improve over 1-5 years; approximately 40-50% of patients at these stages show improvement in attack frequency when exposure ceases. Stage 3v disease rarely reverses significantly once vascular structural changes are established; management goals shift to symptom control and prevention of trophic complications. Stage 4v carries a risk of digital gangrene requiring amputation if not aggressively managed. The sensorineural component (numbness, tingling, reduced VPT) generally does not improve after exposure cessation and may progress slowly. Workers diagnosed at stage 2v or above who continue vibration exposure experience progression in almost all published cohorts. UK industrial injuries legislation recognizes HAVS as a prescribed occupational disease and affected workers may qualify for compensation regardless of whether symptoms improve after exposure cessation.
recoveryTimeline: Attack frequency may stabilize within 3-6 months after removing vibration exposure. Modest improvement in Stockholm staging (e.g., 3v to 2v) may occur over 1-3 years at early stages. Sensorineural changes do not reverse measurably. Pharmacotherapy with nifedipine reduces attack frequency within 2-4 weeks. Fingertip trophic changes at stage 4v require months of vascular treatment and may not fully heal.
complications: Digital gangrene and fingertip amputation at Stockholm Stage 4v — prevented by early specialist referral and aggressive pharmacological vasodilation.
Permanent loss of fine tactile sensation and grip strength impairing the ability to perform manual work, hobbies, and activities of daily living.
Carpal tunnel syndrome and other upper-limb musculoskeletal disorders that frequently co-occur with HAVS from the same vibration exposure.
Occupational disability and inability to return to the usual trade — most stage 3-4v workers cannot safely continue high-vibration roles.
Depression and anxiety secondary to pain, functional loss, and occupational uncertainty — assess and treat proactively alongside physical management.
Failure of compensation claims if formal Stockholm staging and exposure documentation are not obtained early in the disease course.

supportResources.name: UK Health and Safety Executive — HAVS
supportResources.description: HSE employer and worker guidance on vibration exposure limits, health surveillance, and legal obligations under the Control of Vibration at Work Regulations 2005.

supportResources.name: NIOSH — Hand-Arm Vibration
supportResources.description: NIOSH fact sheets and research on hand-arm vibration exposure, HAVS, and prevention in US workplaces.

supportResources.name: Scleroderma & Raynaud UK (SRUK)
supportResources.description: Patient charity with resources on Raynaud-type vasospasm management that overlap with VWF symptom control strategies.

supportResources.name: Faculty of Occupational Medicine — HAVS Guidance
supportResources.description: UK clinical guidance documents on HAVS assessment, staging, and fitness-for-work determination published by the Faculty of Occupational Medicine.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary Raynaud phenomenon
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary Raynaud occurs in young women without vibration exposure, typically involves all fingers symmetrically, and has a benign course with normal nail-fold capillaroscopy and normal VPT testing. VWF is occupational, often asymmetric (dominant hand first), associated with reduced VPT, and may progress despite treatment. Compensation entitlement depends on demonstrating occupational causation absent in primary Raynaud.

confusedWithConditions.name: Carpal tunnel syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Carpal tunnel syndrome causes tingling and numbness in the median nerve distribution (thumb, index, middle, and radial half of the ring finger) with positive Tinel and Phalen signs. It does not cause blanching or vasospastic attacks. However, CTS frequently co-occurs with HAVS from shared vibration exposure; both conditions must be assessed and treated together.

confusedWithConditions.name: Occupational contact dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Occupational dermatitis causes skin inflammation, redness, and scaling from chemical or physical irritants; it does not produce episodic blanching, vasospasm, or neurological changes. Dermatitis may coexist with HAVS in workers exposed to both vibration and chemical agents. Patch testing distinguishes dermatitis from the trophic skin changes of stage 4 VWF.

relatedConditions.name: Raynaud phenomenon
relatedConditions.relevance: Primary and secondary Raynaud share the vasospastic mechanism and treatment approach with VWF; differentiating occupational from idiopathic vasospasm is essential for prognosis and compensation.

relatedConditions.name: Carpal tunnel syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently co-occurs with VWF in vibration-tool users; shared median nerve compression at the wrist from vibration trauma.

relatedConditions.name: Noise-induced hearing loss
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs with HAVS in workers using loud vibrating tools such as chainsaws and jackhammers; both are NIOSH-targeted occupational health outcomes in the same workforce.

relatedConditions.name: Work-related musculoskeletal disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Vibration exposure causes both vascular HAVS and musculoskeletal tendinopathy, joint stiffness, and reduced grip strength as parallel pathways of harm.

faqs.question: What is vibration white finger?
faqs.answer: Vibration white finger (VWF) is an occupational vascular disorder caused by prolonged use of vibrating tools. It produces episodic finger blanching, pain, and numbness triggered by cold or stress. It is the vascular component of hand-arm vibration syndrome (HAVS) and is a prescribed occupational disease in the UK entitling affected workers to compensation.

faqs.question: What causes vibration white finger?
faqs.answer: VWF is caused by repeated vibration from powered hand tools — chainsaws, grinders, pneumatic drills, road breakers, riveters — which damages the small arteries and nerves in the fingers. Vibration causes smooth-muscle hypertrophy and endothelial dysfunction in digital vessels, producing episodic vasospasm. Cold environments, tobacco smoking, and sustained high grip force accelerate disease onset.

faqs.question: What are the stages of vibration white finger?
faqs.answer: VWF is graded 0-4 on the Stockholm Workshop Scale. Stage 0: no symptoms. Stage 1: occasional fingertip blanching. Stage 2: intermittent blanching of middle phalanges. Stage 3: frequent blanching of all phalanges with functional impairment. Stage 4: continuous attacks with trophic skin changes, ulceration, or gangrene.

faqs.question: Is vibration white finger the same as HAVS?
faqs.answer: No, but VWF is the vascular component of HAVS (hand-arm vibration syndrome). HAVS also includes a sensorineural component (numbness, tingling, reduced vibration sense) and a musculoskeletal component (reduced grip, tendinopathy). Workers often have all three components simultaneously, graded separately on the Stockholm scale.

faqs.question: Can vibration white finger be cured?
faqs.answer: Established VWF vascular and sensorineural changes cannot be reversed. Stopping vibration exposure at stage 1-2 stabilizes or modestly improves symptoms in 40-50% of workers over 1-3 years. Stage 3-4 disease generally does not regress. Treatment focuses on reducing attack frequency with calcium-channel blockers and preventing trophic complications.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for vibration white finger?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is removing the worker from vibrating-tool use and stopping smoking. Calcium-channel blockers — nifedipine 10-30 mg daily or amlodipine 5-10 mg daily — reduce vasospastic attack frequency by 50-60%. Topical glyceryl trinitrate treats individual attacks. Severe cases may require iloprost infusion or surgical sympathectomy.

faqs.question: What tools cause vibration white finger?
faqs.answer: The highest-risk tools include chainsaws, pneumatic road breakers, angle grinders, needle guns, rivet hammers, and percussive drills. Vibration white finger also occurs with concrete vibrators, compactors, and some dental and surgical instruments. Risk depends on daily duration of use, tool vibration magnitude, and years of exposure.

faqs.question: How is vibration white finger diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a structured occupational history documenting vibration exposure plus clinical tests: cold provocation test (finger blood pressure drop after 10°C water immersion), vibrotactile perception threshold testing, and two-point discrimination. A Stockholm stage is assigned. Blood tests and nail-fold capillaroscopy exclude connective tissue disease.

faqs.question: Can I claim compensation for vibration white finger?
faqs.answer: Yes, in the UK, VWF is a prescribed industrial injury under the Social Security (Industrial Injuries) Act, entitling workers to disablement benefit if exposure occurred in qualifying occupations. Claims require formal Stockholm staging by an approved occupational physician and documented occupational vibration exposure history.

faqs.question: How long does it take to develop vibration white finger?
faqs.answer: VWF typically develops after 5-10 years of regular vibrating-tool use, but can appear within 2-3 years in workers using old high-magnitude tools or working in very cold conditions for long daily durations. Earlier onset correlates with higher daily vibration dose (A(8) above 5 m/s²) and tobacco smoking.

faqs.question: Does smoking make vibration white finger worse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Nicotine promotes digital vasoconstriction through sympathetic stimulation and endothelial dysfunction, lowering the threshold for vibration-induced vascular damage. Smokers develop VWF at lower vibration doses and progress faster than non-smokers. Smoking cessation is the most impactful single modifiable intervention in VWF management.

faqs.question: Can women get vibration white finger?
faqs.answer: Yes. Women in comparable vibration-tool roles develop HAVS at rates similar to men. The apparent male predominance in published cases reflects occupational exposure patterns rather than biological resistance. Women in dental practice, assembly work, and food processing with vibration exposure are increasingly diagnosed with HAVS.

faqs.question: Are anti-vibration gloves effective?
faqs.answer: Anti-vibration gloves reduce vibration transmission by 5-15% at high frequencies but do not reliably attenuate the low frequencies most damaging to digital vessels. They do not substitute for tool substitution or reduced exposure duration, but may contribute marginally to dose reduction. They also protect against cold, which independently worsens vasospasm.

faqs.question: What is the difference between vibration white finger and Raynaud disease?
faqs.answer: Primary Raynaud disease occurs without vibration exposure, predominantly in young women, affects all fingers symmetrically, and has a benign course. VWF is occupational, often involves the dominant hand first, is associated with sensorineural changes (reduced vibration sense), and may progress to trophic skin damage. VWF entitles workers to compensation; primary Raynaud does not.

faqs.question: How often should vibration-tool workers be health-screened?
faqs.answer: The UK Control of Vibration at Work Regulations 2005 requires employers to offer health surveillance to all workers exposed above the action value (2.5 m/s² A(8)), including structured symptom questionnaires and clinical assessment at baseline and at least every 2 years, annually for high-exposure workers or those showing early symptoms.

metaTitle: Vibration White Finger: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Vibration white finger (HAVS): episodic finger blanching in vibrating-tool users. Stockholm staging, diagnosis, nifedipine and sympathectomy treatment reviewed.
